content:
Passive RF techniques for identifying nearby drone activity.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Generated by GeminiThe first time I tried to detect a drone with an RTL-SDR, I expected it to be easy.
I had seen countless YouTube videos showing beautiful waterfall displays with obvious signals appearing the moment a drone powered on. It looked as though all I needed was an RTL-SDR, an antenna, and a laptop.
Reality was much less exciting.
When I tuned into the 2.4 GHz ISM band, I wasn’t greeted by a neat drone signature. I was looking at what can only be described as RF chaos. Wi-Fi access points were everywhere. Bluetooth devices appeared in short bursts. Wireless keyboards, headphones, smart home devices, and even my microwave contributed to the noise floor.
The drone wasn’t invisible.
It was simply one transmitter among hundreds.
That was the moment I realised building a drone detection system wasn’t really about receiving RF signals.
It was about separating meaningful signals from everything else occupying the spectrum.
My first assumption was completely wrong
